infrastructure.narrative: India's digital acceptance infrastructure is bifurcating sharply: UPI QR codes hit 770.8M in Apr 2026 (up 9.6M, the highest monthly gain since Feb), while POS terminals fell to 11.45M (down 322K, erasing the entire Dec–Mar recovery). The UPI-to-POS ratio widened to 67×, the highest on record, as UPI QR scaled 16.4% YoY and POS growth collapsed to 1.5% YoY — the slowest rate in 16 periods. The UPI-to-Bharat QR ratio hit 137×, confirming Bharat QR is obsolete. Lenders financing merchant acceptance should allocate exclusively to UPI QR infrastructure — it is the only channel with sustained expansion, while POS is in structural decline with no recovery signal.

atm-offsite-abs.title: Offsite ATMs at 73,857 — first monthly gain in 16 periods
atm-offsite-abs.observation: Offsite ATM count stands at 73,857 in Apr 2026, up 164 units from Mar's 73,693.
atm-offsite-abs.direction: Rose 73,693 → 73,857 in Apr — the first monthly gain since Dec 2024, breaking a 15-period contraction streak.
chain: Offsite ATMs rose 164 units in Apr 2026 to 73,857, the first monthly gain in 16 periods.

The Apr gain breaks a 15-month contraction streak but does not reverse the structural decline — the network is still 20% below its Jan 2024 level.
A single-month recovery does not signal stabilisation when the network has lost 20% of capacity in 27 months.
atm-offsite-abs.inference: The Apr gain is a temporary pause in a terminal decline — offsite ATMs remain at their lowest level on record, down 20% from Jan 2024. Banks continue closing unprofitable locations as digital channels absorb transaction volumes. One month of growth does not reverse the structural shift away from offsite infrastructure.

atm-offsite-yoy.direction: YoY decline improved -14.0% → -12.9% → -11.8% → -10.5% over four months, with Apr at -10.5% marking the best reading since Jan 2025.
chain: Offsite ATM contraction improved to -10.5% YoY in Apr 2026, the fourth consecutive monthly improvement and the best reading in 15 months.
The YoY rate improved 3.5 points from Jan's -14.0%, driven by favorable base effects as the closure wave peaked in early 2025.
The moderation confirms the worst of the contraction is behind — the decline is slowing as the network approaches a stable floor.
However, the network is still shrinking at double-digit rates, and the absolute count remains at its historical minimum.
atm-offsite-yoy.inference: The offsite ATM contraction is moderating as expected — the YoY rate improved 3.5 points in four months, driven by base effects as the 2025 closure wave rolls off. The decline will continue improving toward single digits by mid-2026, but the network will not return to prior levels. Banks have permanently exited unprofitable locations.

atm-onsite-abs.inference: Onsite ATM expansion is accelerating — banks added 2,041 units in four months, the fastest pace in this window, confirming branch-based terminals are a strategic priority. The network is at its highest level on record and will continue growing as offsite closures redirect capital to high-traffic locations. Branch ATMs are the winning infrastructure model.

atm-onsite-yoy.title: Onsite ATMs +3.7% YoY — growth re-accelerating after Mar dip

atm-onsite-yoy.inference: Onsite ATM growth is accelerating — the YoY rate rose 1.5 points from Feb to Apr, confirming the expansion pace is picking up as banks prioritise branch-based terminals. Growth will continue accelerating toward 4% as the network reaches new highs. Branch ATMs are the only growing segment of physical infrastructure.

The network is now 130× smaller than UPI QR and at its lowest level on record. Banks are abandoning the failed standard as UPI QR dominates merchant acceptance. The contraction will continue.

pos-category-share-scan.inference: PSBs regained POS share in Apr after Mar's pullback — the 0.5-point gain confirms PSBs are expanding terminals faster than private banks, driven by government-backed financial inclusion mandates. pos-terminals-abs.title: POS terminals fell to 11.45M — 322K-unit drop erases recovery
pos-terminals-abs.observation: POS terminal count stands at 11,446,998 in Apr 2026, down 322,363 units from 11,769,361 in Mar 2026.
pos-terminals-abs.direction: Fell 11,769,361 → 11,446,998, erasing the entire 294,689-unit recovery built from Dec 2025 to Mar 2026 and resetting the network below Dec's low of 11,474,672.
chain: The network lost 322,363 terminals in Apr 2026, the largest single-month decline since Nov 2025's 1.05M-unit collapse.

The network now sits 861,243 units below Oct 2025's peak of 12,308,241, with no consecutive growth periods remaining.
pos-terminals-abs.inference: The Apr correction confirms the Dec–Mar recovery was temporary stabilisation, not durable expansion. The network has now given back every unit gained in Q1 2026 and sits at its lowest level since Nov 2025. Lenders relying on POS acceptance infrastructure should treat this as a structural contraction signal — the network is shrinking, not consolidating.

pos-terminals-yoy.title: POS terminals at 1.5% YoY — slowest growth in 16-period window
pos-terminals-yoy.observation: POS terminals grew 1.45% year-on-year in Apr 2026, down from 6.05% in Mar 2026.
pos-terminals-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 6.0% → 1.5%, a 4.6-point drop — the rate has fallen from Jan's 11.5% peak and now sits at the minimum level in the 16-period series.
chain: The 1.5% YoY rate represents just 163,252 net units added over 12 months, down from Mar's 670,000-unit annual gain.
The deceleration is driven by both a high base (Apr 2025's 11,283,266 terminals) and Apr 2026's 322K-unit monthly decline.
The rate has compressed from 31.5% in May 2025 to 1.5% in Apr 2026, a 30-point collapse in 11 months.
pos-terminals-yoy.inference: The 1.5% YoY rate is the weakest reading in this window and signals the POS network is near stall speed. The combination of a high base and Apr's sharp monthly decline has pushed annual growth to near-zero. If the network posts another monthly decline in May, the YoY rate will turn negative for the first time in this dataset. Lenders should treat POS acceptance infrastructure as a contracting channel.

upi-bharat-qr-ratio.title: UPI QR codes at 137× per Bharat QR — divergence widening
upi-bharat-qr-ratio.observation: There are now 137.3 UPI QR codes for every Bharat QR code in Apr 2026, up 2.12 from 135.1 in Mar 2026.
upi-bharat-qr-ratio.direction: Ratio rose 135.1× → 137.3×, the highest level on record — the gap has widened by 2.1 points in one month, the sharpest move since Mar's 7.2-point jump.
chain: The 137.3× ratio means UPI QR deployment is outpacing Bharat QR by 136 to 1 — for every Bharat QR code added, 137 UPI QR codes are deployed.
The ratio has risen from 53.5× in Jan 2024 to 137.3× in Apr 2026, a 2.6× increase in 28 months.
The widening gap confirms Bharat QR is being abandoned in favour of UPI QR as the dominant acceptance standard.
upi-bharat-qr-ratio.inference: The 137× ratio is a structural divergence signal — Bharat QR is effectively obsolete, with UPI QR capturing all incremental deployment. Lenders financing merchant acceptance infrastructure should focus exclusively on UPI QR-enabled solutions; Bharat QR has no growth trajectory. The ratio will continue widening as UPI QR scales and Bharat QR stagnates or contracts.

upi-qr-abs.title: UPI QR codes hit 770.8M — 9.6M-code gain, highest on record

chain: The 9.6M-code gain in Apr 2026 is 37% larger than Mar's 7.0M and confirms the network has re-accelerated after Mar's slowdown.

At 770.8M codes, the network is now 2.4× the size it was in Jan 2024 (321.4M) and shows no signs of saturation.
upi-qr-abs.inference: UPI QR deployment is in full expansion mode — the 9.6M-code gain confirms Mar's 7.0M slowdown was a one-month pause, not a trend break. The network is on track to exceed 780M codes by May 2026, with monthly gains consistently in the 7–10M range. Lenders should treat UPI QR as the dominant acceptance infrastructure — it is scaling faster than POS terminals are contracting, and the gap is widening every month.

upi-qr-per-pos.inference: The 67× ratio confirms the two channels are on opposite trajectories — UPI QR is scaling exponentially while POS is contracting. Lenders financing merchant acceptance should allocate capital exclusively to UPI QR infrastructure; POS terminals are a shrinking asset class with no recovery signal. The ratio will continue widening as UPI QR adds 7–10M codes monthly and POS struggles to stabilise.

upi-qr-yoy.title: UPI QR codes at 16.4% YoY — stable expansion trajectory
upi-qr-yoy.observation: UPI QR codes grew 16.37% year-on-year in Apr 2026, up from 15.70% in Mar 2026.
upi-qr-yoy.direction: YoY growth accelerated 15.7% → 16.4%, a 0.7-point gain — the rate has stabilised in the 15–17% range across the 16-period series, with Apr's 16.4% near the median.
chain: The 16.4% YoY rate represents 108.4M net codes added over 12 months, in line with the structural 100M+ annual expansion trajectory.

upi-qr-yoy.inference: The 16.4% YoY rate confirms UPI QR deployment is in a stable, high-growth phase — the network is adding 100M+ codes annually with no signs of saturation. The rate has held in the 15–17% range for 12 consecutive months, even as the base has risen from 662M to 770M codes. Lenders should treat UPI QR as the primary acceptance infrastructure for digital payments — it is the only channel with sustained double-digit growth and structural expansion momentum.

cards_stock.narrative: Debit card share shifted sharply in Apr 2026 — PSBs jumped 1.29pp to 64.17% (the largest gain on record) while privates rose 0.69pp to 25.84% (also a record). Both moves are 7–13× the typical monthly change and coincide exactly, pointing to a one-time stock rebalancing event (likely inactive card purges) rather than a structural shift in issuance momentum. PSBs have recovered all share lost since Oct 2024, erasing 10 months of erosion in a single period, while privates hit their highest level in 28 periods. The next two periods will determine whether this is a permanent reset or a transient adjustment: if both hold their new levels, the 16-month equilibrium (PSBs 63.0%–64.5%, privates 25.0%–25.4%) has structurally broken; if they revert, Apr 2026 was a cleanup event and the prior trends remain intact.
